md: Two datasets can have the same mean and look completely different. Compare $48, 50, 52$ to $0, 50, 100$ — same mean (50), but the second varies wildly. **Measures of spread** capture how stretched out a dataset is. We'll cover four: **range**, **IQR**, **variance**, and **standard deviation**.
**Range = max − min.** Easy to compute, but uses only two numbers. One outlier and the range can balloon.
Example: $5, 7, 9, 12, 100$. Range = $100 - 5 = 95$ — driven entirely by the outlier.
Sort the data. The **five-number summary** is: **min, Q1, median, Q3, max**.
**Q1 (first quartile):** median of the lower half (25th percentile).
**Q3 (third quartile):** median of the upper half (75th percentile).
**Interquartile range (IQR):** $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$. Captures the spread of the middle 50% of the data.
**Big advantage:** IQR ignores the outermost 25% on each side, so it's robust to outliers.
Lower half: $4, 6, 7, 8$. Median of lower half = $(6+7)/2 = 6.5$ → **Q1 = 6.5**.
These capture **typical distance from the mean**. Standard deviation is the gold standard for spread when data is approximately symmetric.
**Step 2.** For each value, compute $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ — the squared deviation.

blocks.title: Population vs sample
blocks.body: When working with a **sample** (a subset of the population), we usually divide by $n - 1$ instead of $n$ for variance ('Bessel's correction'). For high-school intro stats with the full data, $n$ is fine. The two only matter when you're inferring about a larger population.

md: ### Worked example — std dev of a small dataset

blocks.title: Why squared?
blocks.body: If we just averaged $x_i - \bar{x}$, the positives and negatives would cancel — average is always exactly 0. Squaring makes everything positive (and weights big deviations more heavily). Taking the square root at the end gives a result in the original units.

blocks.title: When to use which
blocks.body: **Range:** quick eyeball check. Sensitive to outliers. **IQR:** robust spread for skewed data; pairs naturally with the median. **Standard deviation:** the workhorse for symmetric, well-behaved data; pairs with the mean. Together with the mean (or median), spread completes the picture: 'where is the data, and how spread out is it?'

md: ## 5. See the spread measures in action

The widget below shows a dataset as a dot plot and computes every measure of center AND spread (range, Q1, Q3, IQR, SD, MAD). Switch to the **'With one outlier'** preset and compare how the SD changes vs. the IQR — that's the robustness contrast in numbers.
